reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Late May is the part of the year where nothing is announcing itself. The big spring milestones have passed, summer hasn't arrived. It's just Thursday. The grass needs cutting. You have emails to answer. Dinner is whatever's in the fridge. This is where sobriety actually lives — not in the dramatic anniversaries or the cinematic rock-bottom flashbacks, but in the unremarkable middle of a week in May.

When I was drinking, ordinary days were the enemy. They were what I drank at. A Tuesday with nothing happening felt like a small punishment, and the cure was always at the bottom of something. What I didn't understand then was that the ordinary days are the entire game. The trips, the powder days, the weddings, the wins — those are the highlight reel. The ordinary Thursday is the practice. And whoever you are on the practice days is who you actually are.

Will Durant wrote that line about Aristotle: we become what we repeatedly do. Sobriety, eleven years in, has mostly been a long series of unremarkable choices made on unremarkable days. Make the coffee. Answer the text. Go to the meeting or the gym or the walk. Don't pick up. Repeat. The life I have now wasn't built on the big moments. It was built on showing up for the boring ones until they stopped feeling boring and started feeling like mine.
reflection.meditation: What would it mean to treat today — this ordinary Thursday — as the real work, not the warm-up?

affirmations: I am building my life in the ordinary hours, not waiting for the dramatic ones.
Today I have agency over what I do next, regardless of how I feel right now.
I am becoming someone I can actually live with.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Thursday that asks nothing dramatic of me. Grateful for a clear head to notice it, a body that works, and the quiet knowledge that I used to dread days like this and now I get to just have them. The ordinary is the gift I couldn't see before.
gratitude.closing: One more day, on its own terms.
